title: Some of It, Not All of It

pattern.title: The partitive: kahvia, vettä, kaks pullaa
pattern.summary: The partitive marks a part rather than a whole, and it turns up far more often than learners expect: anything uncountable (kahvia, vettä), anything after a number above one (kaks pullaa), anything you're partway through, and everything you negate. There's no English equivalent to translate from. Endings are -a/-ä, or -ta/-tä after a consonant - and in speech an -a after a vowel usually just lengthens it: kahvia → kahvii, maitoa → maitoo.
examples: Mä juon kahvii joka aamu. = I drink coffee every morning.
Mä otan kaks pullaa. = I'll take two buns.
Mul ei oo rahaa. = I don't have any money.

glosses.kahvii: coffee - partitive 'kahvia', spoken as kahvii. Coffee is a mass, so it's partitive by default

glosses.onks: is there / do you have - spoken 'onko'
glosses.maitoo: milk - partitive 'maitoa'. Asking for an amount, not one specific carton

glosses.pullaa: bun - partitive singular. After any number but one, Finnish uses the singular partitive, never a plural

glosses.rahaa: money - partitive 'rahaa'. Negation always takes the partitive

glosses.vähän: a little - quantity words pull the partitive after them

sentences.en: I'm waiting for you in front of the library.

glosses.odotan: am waiting for - odottaa ALWAYS takes the partitive, whatever you mean

sentences.en: Could I get a bit more water?

glosses.vettä: water - partitive of 'vesi'. An everyday irregular worth knowing cold

glosses.juo: drink - bare stem after the negative
glosses.olutta: beer - partitive 'olutta'. Consonant-final words take -ta/-tä, not plain -a

glosses.on: have - 'meil on' is the spoken 'we have'
glosses.lasta: child - partitive of 'lapsi'. Another everyday irregular: lapsi → lasta

glosses.kirjaa: book - partitive, because you're partway through it. 'Mä luen kirjan' would mean you'll finish it
